title: Solve quadratic equations: complex solutions
description: Solve quadratic equations using the quadratic formula. Some of the equations have real solutions while others have complex solutions.
topic_path: Module 1: Polynomial, rational, and radical relationships
Topic D: Lessons 38-40: Complex roots and the fundamental theorem of algebra

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $x^2-5x-10=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=1}, \pink{b=-5}$, and $\green{c=-10}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{-5})^2-4( \blue{1})(\green {-10}) \\ \\&=25+40\\ \\&=65\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are  real if the discriminant is $\geq0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two real solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two real solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{-5})\pm\sqrt{65}}{2(\blue{1})}\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{5\pm\sqrt{65}}{2} \\\\\\
&=\dfrac{5}{2}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{65}}{2}
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $\dfrac{5}{2}+\dfrac{\sqrt{65}}{2}  $

- $\dfrac{5}{2}-\dfrac{\sqrt{65}}{2}  $

**These can be represented by the single expression $\dfrac{5}{2}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{65}}{2}  $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $-5x^2+x+2=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=-5}, \pink{b=1}$, and $\green{c=2}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{1})^2-4( \blue{-5})(\green {2}) \\ \\&=1+40\\ \\&=41\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are  real if the discriminant is $\geq0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two real solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two real solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{1})\pm\sqrt{41}}{2(\blue{-5})}\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{-1\pm\sqrt{41}}{-10}\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{-1}{-10}\pm-\dfrac{\sqrt{41}}{10}\\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{10}\mp\dfrac{\sqrt{41}}{10}
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $\dfrac{1}{10}+\dfrac{\sqrt{41}}{10}  $

- $ \dfrac{1}{10}-\dfrac{\sqrt{41}}{10} $

**These can be represented by the single expression $  \dfrac{1}{10}\mp\dfrac{\sqrt{41}}{10}$.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $2x^2+6x-2=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=2}, \pink{b=6}$, and $\green{c=-2}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{6})^2-4( \blue{2})(\green {-2}) \\ \\&=36+16\\ \\&=52\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are  real if the discriminant is $\geq0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two real solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two real solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{6})\pm\sqrt{52}}{2(\blue{2})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-6\pm \sqrt{52}}{4}\\ \\ \\&=\dfrac{-6\pm 2\sqrt{13}}{4}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{52}\,\text{ as }\,\,  2\sqrt{13} \\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-6}{4}\pm\dfrac{2\sqrt{13}}{4}\\\\\\
&=-\dfrac{3}{2}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{13}}{2}
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $-\dfrac{3}{2}+\dfrac{\sqrt{13}}{2}  $

- $ -\dfrac{3}{2}-\dfrac{\sqrt{13}}{2} $

**These can be represented by the single expression $-\dfrac{3}{2}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{13}}{2}  $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $-9x^2+x+3=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=-9}, \pink{b=1}$, and $\green{c=3}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{1})^2-4( \blue{-9})(\green {3})\\ \\&=1+108\\ \\&=109\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are  real if the discriminant is $\geq0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two real solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two real solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{1})\pm\sqrt{109}}{2(\blue{-9})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-1\pm\sqrt{109}}{-18}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-1}{-18}\pm-\dfrac{\sqrt{109}}{18}\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{18}\mp\dfrac{\sqrt{109}}{18}
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $ \dfrac{1}{18}+\dfrac{\sqrt{109}}{18} $

- $ \dfrac{1}{18}-\dfrac{\sqrt{109}}{18} $

**These can be represented by the single expression $ \dfrac{1}{18}\mp\dfrac{\sqrt{109}}{18} $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $11x^2-6x-2=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=11}, \pink{b=-6}$, and $\green{c=-2}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{-6})^2-4( \blue{11})(\green {-2}) \\ \\&=36+88\\ \\&=124\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are  real if the discriminant is $\geq0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two real solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two real solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{-6})\pm\sqrt{124}}{2(\blue{11})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{6\pm2\sqrt{31}}{22}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{124}\,\text{ as }\,\,  2\sqrt{31} \\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{6}{22}\pm\dfrac{2\sqrt{31}}{22} \\\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{11}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{31}}{11}
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $ \dfrac{3}{11}+\dfrac{\sqrt{31}}{11} $

- $\dfrac{3}{11}-\dfrac{\sqrt{31}}{11}  $

**These can be represented by the single expression $ \dfrac{3}{11}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{31}}{11} $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $6x^2-6x-2=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=6}, \pink{b=-6}$, and $\green{c=-2}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{-6})^2-4( \blue{6})(\green {-2}) \\ \\&=36+48\\ \\&=84\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are  real if the discriminant is $\geq0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two real solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two real solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{-6})\pm\sqrt{84}}{2(\blue{6})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{6\pm2\sqrt{21}}{12}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{84}\,\text{ as }\,\, 2 \sqrt{21} \\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{6}{12}\pm\dfrac{2\sqrt{21}}{12} \\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{2}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{21}}{6}
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $ \dfrac{1}{2}+\dfrac{\sqrt{21}}{6} $

- $ \dfrac{1}{2}-\dfrac{\sqrt{21}}{6} $

**These can be represented by the single expression $ \dfrac{1}{2}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{21}}{6} $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $-3x^2+4x+1=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=-3}, \pink{b=4}$, and $\green{c=1}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

$\begin{align}
\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{4})^2-4( \blue{-3})(\green {1}) \\ \\
&=16+12 \\ \\
&=28
\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are  real if the discriminant is $\geq0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two real solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two real solutions:

> $\begin{align}
x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{4})\pm\sqrt{28}}{2(\blue{-3})} \\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-4\pm2\sqrt{7}}{-6} \qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{28}\,\text{ as }\,\, 2\sqrt{7}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-4}{-6}\pm-\dfrac{2\sqrt{7}}{6} \\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{2}{3}\mp\dfrac{\sqrt{7}}{3}
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $\dfrac{2}{3}+\dfrac{\sqrt{7}}{3}  $

- $ \dfrac{2}{3}-\dfrac{\sqrt{7}}{3} $

**These can be represented by the single expression $ \dfrac{2}{3}\mp\dfrac{\sqrt{7}}{3} $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $2x^2-8x-9=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=2}, \pink{b=-8}$, and $\green{c=-9}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{-8})^2-4( \blue{2})(\green {-9}) \\ \\&=64+72\\ \\&=136\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are  real if the discriminant is $\geq0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two real solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two real solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{-8})\pm\sqrt{136}}{2(\blue{2})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{8\pm2\sqrt{34}}{4}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{136}\,\text{ as }\,\,  2\sqrt{34} \\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{8}{4}\pm\dfrac{2\sqrt{34}}{4}\\ \\ \\
&=2\pm \dfrac{\sqrt{34}}{2} 
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $2+ \dfrac{\sqrt{34}}{2}   $

- $2- \dfrac{\sqrt{34}}{2}   $

**These can be represented by the single expression $2\pm \dfrac{\sqrt{34}}{2}   $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $3x^2-5x+1=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=3}, \pink{b=-5}$, and $\green{c=1}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{-5})^2-4( \blue3)(\green 1) \\ \\&=25-12\\ \\&=13\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are  real if the discriminant is $\geq0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two real solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two real solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{-5})\pm\sqrt{13}}{2(\blue{3})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{5\pm \sqrt{13}}{6} \\\\\\
&=\dfrac{5}{6}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{13}}{6}
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $\dfrac{5}{6}+\dfrac{\sqrt{13}}{6}  $

- $ \dfrac{5}{6}-\dfrac{\sqrt{13}}{6} $

**These can be represented by the single expression $ \dfrac{5}{6}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{13}}{6} $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $2x^2+3x-11=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=2}, \pink{b=3}$, and $\green{c=-11}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{3})^2-4( \blue{2})(\green {-11}) \\ \\&=9+88\\ \\&=97\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are  real if the discriminant is $\geq0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two real solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two real solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{3})\pm\sqrt{97}}{2(\blue{2})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-3\pm\sqrt{97}}{4} \\\\\\
&=-\dfrac{3}{4}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{97}}{4}
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $-\dfrac{3}{4}+\dfrac{\sqrt{97}}{4}  $

- $-\dfrac{3}{4}-\dfrac{\sqrt{97}}{4}  $

**These can be represented by the single expression $ -\dfrac{3}{4}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{97}}{4}$.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $3x^2-2x+4=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=3}, \pink{b=-2}$, and $\green{c=4}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{-2})^2-4( \blue3)(\green 4) \\ \\&=4-48\\ \\&=-44\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are complex if the discriminant is $<0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two complex solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two complex solutions:

>$\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{-2})\pm \sqrt{-44}}{2(\blue{3})}\\\\
&=\dfrac{2\pm i\sqrt{44}}{6} \qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{-44}\,\text{ as }\,\,i\sqrt{44}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{2\pm i\cdot2\sqrt{11}}{6} \qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{44}\,\text{ as }\,\,2\sqrt{11}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{2}{6}\pm\dfrac{i\cdot2\sqrt{11}}{6}\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{3}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{11}}{3}i
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $  \dfrac{1}{3}+\dfrac{\sqrt{11}}{3}i$

- $\dfrac{1}{3}-\dfrac{\sqrt{11}}{3}i  $

**These can be represented by the single expression $\dfrac{1}{3}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{11}}{3}i  $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $7x^2-2x+3=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=7}, \pink{b=-2}$, and $\green{c=3}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{-2})^2-4( \blue{7})(\green {3}) \\ \\&=4-84\\ \\&=-80\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are complex if the discriminant is $<0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two complex solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two complex solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{-2})\pm\sqrt{-80}}{2(\blue{7})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{2\pm i\sqrt{80}}{14}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{-80}\,\text{ as }\,\,i\sqrt{80} \\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{2\pm i\cdot4\sqrt{5}}{14}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{80}\,\text{ as }\,\,  4\sqrt{5} \\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{2}{14}\pm\dfrac{i\cdot4\sqrt{5}}{14}\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{7}\pm\dfrac{2\sqrt{5}}{7}i
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $\dfrac{1}{7}+\dfrac{2\sqrt{5}}{7}i  $

- $\dfrac{1}{7}-\dfrac{2\sqrt{5}}{7}i  $

**These can be represented by the single expression $\dfrac{1}{7}\pm\dfrac{2\sqrt{5}}{7}i  $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $-7x^2+2x-1=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=-7}, \pink{b=2}$, and $\green{c=-1}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{2})^2-4( \blue{-7})(\green {-1}) \\ \\&=4-28\\ \\&=-24\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are complex if the discriminant is $<0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two complex solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two complex solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{2})\pm\sqrt{-24}}{2(\blue{-7})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-2\pm i\sqrt{24}}{-14}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{-24}\,\text{ as }\,\,i\sqrt{24} \\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-2\pm i\cdot2\sqrt{6}}{-14}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{24}\,\text{ as }\,\,  2\sqrt{6} \\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-2}{-14}\pm-\dfrac{i\cdot2\sqrt{6}}{14}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{1}{7}\mp\dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{7}i
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $\dfrac{1}{7}+\dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{7}i  $

- $  \dfrac{1}{7}-\dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{7}i$

**These can be represented by the single expression $\dfrac{1}{7}\mp\dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{7}i  $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $5x^2-x+6=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=5}, \pink{b=-1}$, and $\green{c=6}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{-1})^2-4( \blue{5})(\green {6}) \\ \\&=1-120\\ \\&=-119\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are complex if the discriminant is $<0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two complex solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two complex solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{-1})\pm\sqrt{-119}}{2(\blue{5})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{1\pm i\sqrt{119}}{10}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{-119}\,\text{ as }\,\,i\sqrt{119} \\\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{10}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{119}}{10}i
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $  \dfrac{1}{10}+\dfrac{\sqrt{119}}{10}i$

- $ \dfrac{1}{10}-\dfrac{\sqrt{119}}{10}i $

**These can be represented by the single expression $\dfrac{1}{10}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{119}}{10}i  $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $14x^2-11x+10=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=14}, \pink{b=-11}$, and $\green{c=10}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{-11})^2-4( \blue{14})(\green {10}) \\ \\&=121-560\\ \\&=-439\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are complex if the discriminant is $<0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two complex solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two complex solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{-11})\pm\sqrt{-439}}{2(\blue{14})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{11\pm i\sqrt{439}}{28} \qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{-439}\,\text{ as }\,\,i\sqrt{439}\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{11}{28}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{439}}{28}i
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $ \dfrac{11}{28}+\dfrac{\sqrt{439}}{28}i $

- $\dfrac{11}{28}-\dfrac{\sqrt{439}}{28}i  $

**These can be represented by the single expression $\dfrac{11}{28}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{439}}{28}i  $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $3x^2+9x+17=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=3}, \pink{b=9}$, and $\green{c=17}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{9})^2-4( \blue{3})(\green {17}) \\ \\&=81-204\\ \\&=-123\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are complex if the discriminant is $<0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two complex solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two complex solutions:

 >$\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{9})\pm\sqrt{-123}}{2(\blue{3})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-9\pm i\sqrt{123}}{6} \qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{-123}\,\text{ as }\,\,i\sqrt{123}\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{-9}{6}\pm\dfrac{i\sqrt{123}}{6}\\\\\\
&=-\dfrac{3}{2}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{123}}{6}i
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $ -\dfrac{3}{2}+\dfrac{\sqrt{123}}{6}i $

- $-\dfrac{3}{2}-\dfrac{\sqrt{123}}{6}i  $

**These can be represented by the single expression $ -\dfrac{3}{2}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{123}}{6}i $.**

assessment_items.question_content:

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=-1}, \pink{b=3}$, and $\green{c=-3}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{3})^2-4( \blue{-1})(\green{-3}) \\ \\&=9-12\\ \\&=-3\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are complex if the discriminant is $<0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two complex solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two complex solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{3})\pm\sqrt{-3}}{2(\blue{-1})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-3\pm i\sqrt{3}}{-2}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{-3}\,\text{ as }\,\,i\sqrt{3}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-3}{-2}\pm-\dfrac{i\sqrt{3}}{2}\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{2}\mp\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $\dfrac{3}{2}+\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i  $

- $ \dfrac{3}{2}-\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i $

**These can be represented by the single expression $ \dfrac{3}{2}\mp\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $3x^2-4x+2=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=3}, \pink{b=-4}$, and $\green{c=2}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{-4})^2-4( \blue3)(\green 2) \\ \\&=16-24\\ \\&=-8\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are complex if the discriminant is $<0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two complex solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two complex solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{-4})\pm\sqrt{-8}}{2(\blue{3})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{4\pm i\sqrt{8}}{6}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{-8}\,\text{ as }\,\,i\sqrt{8}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{4\pm i\cdot2\sqrt{2}}{6}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{8}\,\text{ as }\,\,  2\sqrt{2} \\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{4}{6}\pm\dfrac{i\cdot2\sqrt{2}}{6}\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{2}{3}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{3}i
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $\dfrac{2}{3}+\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{3}i  $

- $ \dfrac{2}{3}-\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{3}i $

**These can be represented by the single expression $\dfrac{2}{3}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{3}i  $.**

assessment_items.question_content:

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=3}, \pink{b=-4}$, and $\green{c=4}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{-4})^2-4( \blue3)(\green 4) \\ \\&=16-48\\ \\&=-32\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are complex if the discriminant is $<0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two complex solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two complex solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{-4})\pm\sqrt{-32}}{2(\blue{3})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{4\pm i\sqrt{32}}{6}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{-32}\,\text{ as }\,\,i\sqrt{32}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{4\pm i\cdot4\sqrt{2}}{6}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{32}\,\text{ as }\,\, 4 \sqrt{2} \\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{4}{6}\pm\dfrac{i\cdot4\sqrt{2}}{6}\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{2}{3}\pm\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{3}i
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $  \dfrac{2}{3}+\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{3}i$

- $\dfrac{2}{3}-\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{3}i  $

**These can be represented by the single expression $ \dfrac{2}{3}\pm\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{3}i $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $-3x^2+6x-11=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=-3}, \pink{b=6}$, and $\green{c=-11}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{6})^2-4( \blue{-3})(\green {-11}) \\ \\&=36-132\\ \\&=-96\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are complex if the discriminant is $<0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two complex solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two complex solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{6})\pm\sqrt{-96}}{2(\blue{-3})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-6\pm i\sqrt{96}}{-6}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{-96}\,\text{ as }\,\,i\sqrt{96}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-6\pm i\cdot4\sqrt{6}}{-6}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{96}\,\text{ as }\,\,  4\sqrt{6} \\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-6}{-6}\pm-\dfrac{i\cdot4\sqrt{6}}{6}\\ \\ \\
&=1\mp\dfrac{2\sqrt{6}}{3}i
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $ 1+\dfrac{2\sqrt{6}}{3}i $

- $1-\dfrac{2\sqrt{6}}{3}i  $

**These can be represented by the single expression $ 1\mp\dfrac{2\sqrt{6}}{3}i $.**

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{-2})^2-4( \blue{3})(\green {7}) \\ \\&=4-84\\ \\&=-80\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are complex if the discriminant is $<0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two complex solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two complex solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{-2})\pm\sqrt{-80}}{2(\blue{3})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{2\pm i\sqrt{80}}{6}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{-80}\,\text{ as }\,\,i\sqrt{80}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{2\pm i\cdot4\sqrt{5}}{6}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{80}\,\text{ as }\,\,  4\sqrt{5} \\ \\ \\

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $4x^2+10x+13=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=4}, \pink{b=10}$, and $\green{c=13}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{10})^2-4( \blue{4})(\green {13}) \\ \\&=100-208\\ \\&=-108\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are complex if the discriminant is $<0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two complex solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two complex solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{10})\pm\sqrt{-108}}{2(\blue{4})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-10\pm i\sqrt{108}}{8}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{-108}\,\text{ as }\,\,i\sqrt{108}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-10\pm i\cdot6\sqrt{3}}{8}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{108}\,\text{ as }\,\,  6\sqrt{3} \\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-10}{8}\pm\dfrac{i\cdot6\sqrt{3}}{8}\\\\\\
&=-\dfrac{5}{4}\pm\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4}i
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $ -\dfrac{5}{4}+\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4}i $

- $-\dfrac{5}{4}-\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4}i  $

**These can be represented by the single expression $-\dfrac{5}{4}\pm\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4}i  $.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=6}, \pink{b=5}$, and $\green{c=4}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{5})^2-4( \blue 6)(\green 4) \\ \\&=25-96\\ \\&=-71\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are complex if the discriminant is $<0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two complex solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two complex solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{5})\pm\sqrt{-71}}{2(\blue{6})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-5\pm i\sqrt{71}}{12}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{-71}\,\text{ as }\,\,i\sqrt{71}\\\\\\
&=-\dfrac{5}{12}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{71}}{12}i
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $-\dfrac{5}{12}+\dfrac{\sqrt{71}}{12}i  $

- $ -\dfrac{5}{12}-\dfrac{\sqrt{71}}{12}i $

**These can be represented by the single expression $-\dfrac{5}{12}\pm\dfrac{\sqrt{71}}{12}i  $.**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the solutions of the quadratic equation $-9x^2+11x-10=0$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### The strategy

If we have a quadratic equation $\blue{a}x^2+\pink{b}x+\green{c}=0$, the quadratic formula gives the solutions:

$\qquad x=\dfrac{-\pink{b}\pm\sqrt{\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}}}{2\blue{a}}$ .

In this problem, $\blue{a=-9}, \pink{b=11}$, and $\green{c=-10}$. Let’s substitute those values into the quadratic formula to find the solutions.

### Determining whether the solutions are real or complex

First, we can substitute the values into the discriminant $\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}$ to see whether the solutions will be real or complex.

 $\begin{align}\pink{b}^2-4\blue{a}\green{c}&=(\pink{11})^2-4( \blue{-9})(\green {-10}) \\ \\&=121-360\\ \\&=-239\end{align}$

Since finding the solutions involves taking the square root of the discriminant, the solutions are complex if the discriminant is $<0$, as it is in this case. Therefore, we will have two complex solutions.

###Finding the solutions

We can substitute the values for $\blue{a}, \pink{b},$ and $\green{c}$ into the quadratic formula to find the two complex solutions:

> $\begin{align}x&=\dfrac{-(\pink{11})\pm\sqrt{-239}}{2(\blue{-9})}\\ \\ \\
&=\dfrac{-11\pm i\sqrt{239}}{-18}\qquad\text{Rewrite }\sqrt{-239}\,\text{ as }\,\,i\sqrt{239}\\\\
&=\dfrac{-11}{-18}\pm-\dfrac{i\sqrt{239}}{18}\\\\\\
&=\dfrac{11}{18}\mp\dfrac{\sqrt{239}}{18}i
\end{align}$

### Conclusion

The equation has two solutions.

- $\dfrac{11}{18}+\dfrac{\sqrt{239}}{18}i$

- $\dfrac{11}{18}-\dfrac{\sqrt{239}}{18}i$

**These can be represented by the single expression $\dfrac{11}{18}\mp\dfrac{\sqrt{239}}{18}i$.**
